title: Why the Ocean Is Salty
summary: In the days when the world was young, there were two brothers. Centers on Spirit,  human attempt to understand the soul, afterlife, and the unseen forces that govern existence..
full_text:
In the days when the world was young, there were two brothers. The elder was rich beyond counting, with lands that stretched to every horizon and a house full of fine things. The younger was so poor that on the night before Christmas, his cupboards held nothing but dust and cobwebs. With no other choice, the poor brother went to his wealthy sibling and begged for something to eat.

The rich brother sneered but offered him a scrap of ham. "Take it," he said, "but you must earn it. Go to the gates of the underworld and trade this meat for whatever they offer you. Do not come back until you have something of value."

The poor brother, desperate, took the ham and walked into the dark forest. As night fell, he met an old man with a long white beard who sat on a stone by the roadside. "Where are you going?" the old man asked. The poor brother told him. The old man nodded and said, "When you reach the Hall of the Dead, the demons will try to buy your ham with gold and jewels. Do not accept any of it. Demand the little hand-mill that sits behind the door. It looks ordinary, but it can grind anything you desire."

The poor brother thanked the old man and continued until he came to the gates of the underworld. The demons smelled the ham and crowded around him. "We will give you a chest of gold for that meat!" they cried. The poor brother shook his head. "I will only trade for the hand-mill behind the door." The demons argued and pleaded, but he would not relent. At last, they gave him the mill.

He brought it home and set it on the table. "Grind herrings and broth," he said. The mill began to turn, and in moments a steaming bowl of fish and pottage appeared. He ate until his stomach was full, then said, "Grind a warm blanket and a fire." The mill produced them instantly. From that day forward, the poor brother and his wife wanted for nothing.

The rich brother heard of his sibling's fortune and came to see the mill. The poor brother, having drunk too much wine, showed his secret. The rich brother demanded to buy it. After much persuasion, he paid a fortune and carried the mill home. But the poor brother had not told him how to stop it.

"Grind herrings and broth!" the rich brother commanded. The mill ground and ground. Soon the herrings and broth overflowed the kitchen, flooded the dining hall, and poured through the windows into the yard. The rich brother tried to turn the mill backward, but it would not stop. The flood threatened to drown him, and he had to wade through the rising tide to reach his brother's house. "Take it back! Take it back!" he screamed. The poor brother agreed, but only for another payment.

Now the poor brother was wealthier than ever. He built a grand house by the sea, so splendid that its golden roof could be seen from miles away. Ships would sail from distant lands just to see the magic mill. One day, a sea captain came and asked if the mill could grind salt. "Yes," the owner said. The captain, who had spent his life in perilous voyages to salt mines, was desperate to own such a treasure. He begged and bargained until the mill was his.

Without asking how to stop it, the captain sailed out to sea and set the mill on the deck. "Grind salt, quickly and well!" he commanded. The mill churned out salt in a white cascade that filled the ship to the gunwales. The captain tried to stop it, but the handle would not still. The ship groaned and sank beneath the weight. The mill tumbled into the depths, and there it sits to this day, grinding salt forever at the bottom of the ocean. And that is why the sea is salty.
moral: Eternal grief shapes the world.

title: Will Scarlet
summary: Will Scarlet was the most hot-tempered of Robin Hood's Merry Men, a man who wore his anger like a cloak and held a grudge like a treasured possession.
full_text:
Will Scarlet was the most hot-tempered of Robin Hood's Merry Men, a man who wore his anger like a cloak and held a grudge like a treasured possession. He was tall and lean, with sharp features and a shock of red hair that gave him his name, and an equally red temper to match.

Before he joined the Merry Men, Will had been a huntsman in the service of a wealthy nobleman. One day, he caught the nobleman's steward beating an old poacher for stealing a single rabbit to feed his starving children. Will intervened, knocking the steward unconscious. For this act of mercy, Will was branded an outlaw and a price was placed on his head.

Will fled to Sherwood Forest, where Robin Hood found him, hungry and defiant, arguing with a group of Robin's men over a deer. Robin was amused by the young man's spirit and invited him to join the band. Will accepted, but only after challenging Robin to a quarterstaff duel, which Will lost — barely.

In Sherwood, Will was both a blessing and a challenge. He was fiercely loyal to the cause of the poor but possessed a sharp tongue that often got him into trouble. He argued with Little John constantly, each man's pride rubbing against the other's like flint on steel. But when a fight broke out, no one fought harder or more fiercely than Will Scarlet.

His finest moment came when the Sheriff of Nottingham ambushed Robin and a small group of the Merry Men. Outnumbered five to one, Will refused to retreat. He stood shoulder to shoulder with Robin, fighting with a fury that made the sheriff's men hesitate. By the time reinforcements arrived, Will had single-handedly held the line.

Later in life, after the Merry Men scattered, Will Scarlet took service with King Richard the Lionheart and fought in the Crusades. He never returned to Sherwood. But his legend lived on — the red-haired hero whose temper was matched only by his courage.
moral: Mercy can heal the angriest heart.

title: Walu: The Sun Woman
summary: The Sun Woman who lights the world each day—a powerful Dreamtime ancestor.
full_text:
The Tuatha De Danann were not gods in the traditional sense. They were not worshiped in temples or honored with sacrifices. They were a race of supernatural beings who ruled Ireland before the arrival of the Celts. They were tall and beautiful, skilled in magic and warfare, and they possessed four treasures that symbolized their power.

The first treasure was the Spear of Lugh. This spear was alive — it thirsted for battle and could not be kept still. When unsheathed, it roared with a voice like thunder, and its tip glowed with a light that blinded its enemies. No army could stand against it.

The second treasure was the Sword of Nuada. When drawn, it could cut through any armor, any shield, any magic. It was undefeatable in single combat, and it had the power to tell the truth. If a liar held the sword, it would burn his hand.

The third treasure was the Cauldron of the Dagda. This cauldron was inexhaustible — no one left it hungry. It could feed an entire army and still be full. But more than food, the cauldron provided wisdom and inspiration. Those who drank from it received the gift of poetry and prophecy.

The fourth treasure was the Lia Fail, the Stone of Destiny. This stone had the power to recognize the true king of Ireland. When the rightful king stood on it, the stone would roar with joy, audible across all of Ireland. When a false claimant stood on it, the stone would remain silent.

The Tuatha De Danann ruled Ireland for many generations. They built forts, raised standing stones, and established the ancient laws of Ireland. But they were eventually defeated by the Milesians, the ancestors of the modern Irish. Rather than leave Ireland, the Tuatha De Danann retreated into the Sidhe — the fairy mounds. They became the Aos Si, the hidden people, who live beneath the hills and still watch over Ireland.

The treasures of the Tuatha De Danann are scattered across Ireland. The Spear of Lugh is said to be at the Hill of Tara. The Sword of Nuada lies beneath the Boyne River. The Cauldron of the Dagda is hidden in a cave in County Donegal. And the Lia Fail still stands at the Hill of Tara, waiting to roar for the next true king of Ireland.
moral: Light comes to those who wake and work—shadows flee before the moving.

title: Whowie: The Monster of the Murray
summary: Long ago, before the old grandfathers' time, there lived a creature in the Murray River that was more terrible than anything that has lived since.
full_text:
Long ago, before the old grandfathers' time, there lived a creature in the Murray River that was more terrible than anything that has lived since. The Whowie was a monster—seven meters long, with a head like a frog and legs like a goanna. Its eyes blazed red in the darkness.

The Whowie lived in a great cave beneath the riverbank—a cave so deep that no one knew its end. Each night, it would emerge to hunt. It did not hunt animals—it hunted people. Whole villages would vanish in a single night.

The tribes were desperate. They tried to flee—they could not. They tried to fight—they could not. The Whowie was too strong, too fast. Its tail could sweep warriors into the water. Its mouth could swallow a dozen men.

But the water-rat tribe—the clever ones—had a plan. They gathered all the tribes together. They gathered firewood, more than had ever been gathered. They stacked it at the entrance of the Whowies cave.

They lit the fire. They fed it for seven days, smoking the monster in its sleep. The Whowie emerged—blinded, confused, unable to see. The tribes attacked with spears, with axes, with all their strength.

The Whowie died at last, but its body was so large that its trampling made the sandhills of the Riverina. Even today, the land remembers—the hills and dunes are the monster's grave.

The lesson: even the most terrible monster can be defeated by those who work together. The lesson also: some monsters should be left sleeping.

The Whowie may be gone, but the deep waterholes still hold danger—the monsters spirit still watches, waiting for the greedy, the alone.
moral: Even terrible monsters fall before united people.

title: When Mother Troll Took in the King's Washing
summary: Troll Mother and her son Drulle live in a forest cottage where she does laundry for travelers. When a king builds a nearby castle for his queen and newborn princess, the lady-in-waiting hires Troll Mother to wash the royal clothes cheaply so she can pocket the difference. Troll Mother becomes enchanted by the beautiful baby clothes and begins stealing them, using a troll rhyme her son recites during counting to conceal the theft. An orphan girl named Inga, who mends clothes at the castle, is blamed for the missing garments and sent away. She wanders to the trolls' cottage and is taken in, where Drulle becomes infatuated with her. Inga meets the queen's young page in the forest, who believes in her innocence and asks for her hand, but she refuses out of shame. When Troll Mother reveals the stolen baby clothes and her tail becomes loose, Inga realizes she is living with trolls and flees. She marries the page, while Drulle's misery causes him to forget the counting rhyme, leading to exposure of the theft. The trolls abandon their cottage, and years later return the remaining baby clothes. Inga and the page live happily, becoming the royal forester and his wife.
moral: Honesty and kindness prevail over greed and deception.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, translated by Holger Lundbergh, Delacorte Press/S. Lawrence, 1973. Originally published in Swedish as 'När trollmor skötte kungens storbyk' (1914) by Elsa Beskow.

title: What Shall Baby's Name Be?
summary: A well-to-do couple's daughter Peggy finally gets a suitor, but when she goes to the cellar for beer she becomes so preoccupied with what to name her future first baby that she, her mother, and her father all become lost in thought, causing the impatient suitor to leave forever.
full_text: A couple with one daughter, Peggy, despaired that no suitor came despite her large dowry. Finally Peter South-farm arrived to propose. While Peggy was sent to fetch beer from the cellar, she began pondering what to name her first baby. She stood outside the cellar lost in thought. Her mother went to find her and also became absorbed in the naming question. Her father went next and joined them in pondering. Meanwhile the suitor sat alone, grew angry thinking they were mocking him, took his hat and left. As he passed, the father called out they were deciding the name of Peggy's first baby. The suitor replied 'The baby will not be called after me!' and departed, never to return.
moral: Do not let future concerns distract from present opportunities; strike while the iron is hot.
source: Baron G. Djurklou, Fairy Tales from the Swedish (1901), translated by H.L. Brækstad.

title: Why the Executioner is Called Assessor
summary: An executioner on his way to an execution discovers a robbers' den in the forest. After completing his duty, he tells the king, who disguises himself as a tramp and together they defeat twelve robbers. In gratitude, the king decrees that executioners shall be called 'Assessor' and treated with respect.
full_text: An executioner walking to an execution discovered a robbers' den when a woman's head appeared through a trap door. He marked the trees and continued. After performing the execution, he told the king. The king, bold and strong, insisted they two should capture the robbers alone. They disguised themselves as ragged tramps, hid a sword and an axe, and met the twelve robbers in the forest. Claiming to be starving beggars, they were taken to the cave and fed. When the robbers had eaten, the executioner and king pushed the table against the robbers, pinned them to the wall, threw brandy in their eyes, and killed all twelve. The king offered the treasure, but the executioner asked that his profession be made respectable. The king issued an edict that executioners be called 'Assessor' and wear a three-cornered hat, and no one should spit after them.
moral: Every honest profession deserves respect, no matter how unpleasant its duties.
source: Baron G. Djurklou, Fairy Tales from the Swedish (1901), translated by H.L. Brækstad.

title: Why the Bear Is Stumpy-Tailed
summary: In this origin story, a cunning fox tricks a gullible bear into believing he can catch fish by leaving his tail in a frozen pond overnight. The bear's tail freezes solid, and when he tries to pull it out, it snaps off, explaining why bears have short, stumpy tails today.
full_text:
WHY THE BEAR IS STUMPY-TAILED

Long ago, when the animals could speak, the bear had a long, beautiful tail that he was very proud of. He would wave it back and forth, boasting to all the other creatures about its magnificence. The fox, who was as cunning as they come, decided to play a trick on the bear to humble his pride. 

One cold winter's day, the fox approached the bear and said, 'Bruin, have you ever tried the new way of fishing? It is the easiest method in the world. All you have to do is go to the frozen pond, make a small hole in the ice, and sit very still with your tail lowered into the water. The fish are so attracted to a long, furry tail that they will bite onto it in droves. By morning, you will have a tail full of fish!' 

 The bear, eager for an easy meal and blinded by his own greed, followed the fox's advice. He went to the pond, cut a hole in the ice, and lowered his magnificent tail into the freezing water. He sat there for hours, shivering in the cold, but he remained patient, imagining the feast that awaited him. 

As the night grew colder, the water around his tail began to freeze. The ice gripped the fur in a tight, frozen hold. When the sun rose the next morning, the bear decided he had waited long enough. He gave a mighty heave and pulled with all his strength. But instead of a catch of fish, there was a loud snap! The ice had frozen so solidly that the bear's tail snapped off at the root. 

Bruin roared in pain and anger, but the fox only laughed and laughed. From that day on, the bear has had only a short, stumpy tail, as a lasting reminder to never trust a fox's fishing tips.
moral: Do not let pride and greed blind you to the deception of others.

title: Well Done and Ill Paid
summary: A man tricks a bear into being bound to a sledge, but the fox who helped him is betrayed by the man's own wife, who sends hounds to hunt the fox.
full_text: A man is forced to promise his horse to a bear. A cunning fox helps the man trick the bear by pretending to be a marksman in the woods, leading the bear to voluntarily be bound to the man's sledge for 'transport.' Once bound, the man kills the bear. However, when the fox asks for his payment—a fat wether—the man's wife convinces him to send hounds instead. The fox narrowly escapes the hounds, reflecting on how 'well done is often ill paid' and how betrayal can come from one's own home.
moral: Do not trust those who are quick to betray others, as they may eventually betray you.

title: Why the Sea is Salt
summary: A man possesses a magical mill capable of producing any item on command. A curious sailor steals the mill and, not knowing the command to stop it, asks for salt. The mill produces salt relentlessly, filling the ocean and explaining why the sea remains salty to this day.
full_text: Once on a time there was a man who had a mill which could produce anything that was asked of it. He had it from his father, and he lived quite happily with it for many years. One day a sailor came to him and asked to borrow the mill for a short time. The man, being kind, agreed. But the sailor was greedy; he didn't want to give the mill back. He sailed away with it to his own country. When he got there, he wanted to see what the mill could do, so he asked it to produce salt. The mill began to produce salt, and it didn't stop. It produced salt and salt, and salt and salt, until the whole ship was full of salt, and then the salt began to flow into the sea. The sailor didn't know the word to stop the mill, and so it continued to produce salt until the entire ocean became salty, as it is to this very day.
moral: Greed and a lack of knowledge can lead to an uncontrollable and disastrous outcome.

title: Wainamoinen's Sowing
summary: Wainamoinen attempts to sow the earth, but the land remains barren. He is challenged by powerful winds and storms that seek to prevent the growth of life. Using his profound magical songs, he calms the elements and prepares the land for agriculture, establishing the first signs of order and sustenance in the world.
full_text: This legendary tale explores the depths of Finnish folklore. Wainamoinen attempts to sow the earth, but the land remains barren. He is challenged by powerful winds and storms that seek to prevent the growth of life. Using his profound magical songs, he calms the elements and prepares the land for agriculture, establishing the first signs of order and sustenance in the world. The narrative emphasizes the magical nature of the world and the eternal struggle between order and chaos, reflecting the spiritual beliefs of the ancient North.
moral: The balance of nature must be respected.

title: Wainamoinen and Youkahainen
summary: Youkahainen, a young and arrogant singer, challenges the elderly Wainamoinen to a singing match to prove his superiority. Wainamoinen uses his deep knowledge of the origins of things and his superior magical songs to sing Youkahainen into a swamp, demonstrating that ancient wisdom far outweighs youthful arrogance.
full_text: This legendary tale explores the depths of Finnish folklore. Youkahainen, a young and arrogant singer, challenges the elderly Wainamoinen to a singing match to prove his superiority. Wainamoinen uses his deep knowledge of the origins of things and his superior magical songs to sing Youkahainen into a swamp, demonstrating that ancient wisdom far outweighs youthful arrogance. The narrative emphasizes the magical nature of the world and the eternal struggle between order and chaos, reflecting the spiritual beliefs of the ancient North.
moral: Ancient wisdom outweighs youthful arrogance.

title: Wainamoinen's Lamentation
summary: Overcome with grief after Aino's death, Wainamoinen wanders the world, mourning the loss of the woman he loved. Through his songs, he reflects on the nature of love, the pain of loss, and the inevitability of fate, while continuing to refine his magical abilities and search for meaning in his solitude.
full_text: This legendary tale explores the depths of Finnish folklore. Overcome with grief after Aino's death, Wainamoinen wanders the world, mourning the loss of the woman he loved. Through his songs, he reflects on the nature of love, the pain of loss, and the inevitability of fate, while continuing to refine his magical abilities and search for meaning in his solitude. The narrative emphasizes the magical nature of the world and the eternal struggle between order and chaos, reflecting the spiritual beliefs of the ancient North.
moral: True love transcends all boundaries.

title: Wainamoinen's Hapless Journey
summary: Seeking a new bride to end his loneliness, Wainamoinen sails to distant and strange lands. During his journey, he encounters numerous obstacles and faces various trials that test both his physical endurance and his magical prowess, often finding himself in precarious situations that require his ingenuity to survive.
full_text: This legendary tale explores the depths of Finnish folklore. Seeking a new bride to end his loneliness, Wainamoinen sails to distant and strange lands. During his journey, he encounters numerous obstacles and faces various trials that test both his physical endurance and his magical prowess, often finding himself in precarious situations that require his ingenuity to survive. The narrative emphasizes the magical nature of the world and the eternal struggle between order and chaos, reflecting the spiritual beliefs of the ancient North.
moral: The balance of nature must be respected.

title: Wainamoinen's Rescue
summary: While on his journeys, Wainamoinen is captured or trapped in a foreign land, often within the depths of the sea or a dark cave. Through a combination of his own magical songs and the unexpected help of allies, he is eventually rescued, continuing his endless quest for a suitable partner.
full_text: This legendary tale explores the depths of Finnish folklore. While on his journeys, Wainamoinen is captured or trapped in a foreign land, often within the depths of the sea or a dark cave. Through a combination of his own magical songs and the unexpected help of allies, he is eventually rescued, continuing his endless quest for a suitable partner. The narrative emphasizes the magical nature of the world and the eternal struggle between order and chaos, reflecting the spiritual beliefs of the ancient North.
moral: The balance of nature must be respected.

title: Wainamoinen's Boat-building
summary: Wainamoinen decides to sail to Pohjola and spends immense effort building a magnificent boat. However, he fails to complete it because he forgets a small but crucial piece, the cross-beam. This story serves as a lesson on the importance of attention to detail and the dangers of pride.
full_text: This legendary tale explores the depths of Finnish folklore. Wainamoinen decides to sail to Pohjola and spends immense effort building a magnificent boat. However, he fails to complete it because he forgets a small but crucial piece, the cross-beam. This story serves as a lesson on the importance of attention to detail and the dangers of pride. The narrative emphasizes the magical nature of the world and the eternal struggle between order and chaos, reflecting the spiritual beliefs of the ancient North.
moral: The balance of nature must be respected.

title: Wainamoinen Finds the Lost-word
summary: Wainamoinen seeks the 'lost words' of creation, ancient incantations that provide total mastery over nature. He journeys to the deepest parts of the ocean or the underworld to retrieve this forbidden knowledge, emphasizing the theme of knowledge as the ultimate source of power.
full_text: This legendary tale explores the depths of Finnish folklore. Wainamoinen seeks the 'lost words' of creation, ancient incantations that provide total mastery over nature. He journeys to the deepest parts of the ocean or the underworld to retrieve this forbidden knowledge, emphasizing the theme of knowledge as the ultimate source of power. The narrative emphasizes the magical nature of the world and the eternal struggle between order and chaos, reflecting the spiritual beliefs of the ancient North.
moral: Ancient wisdom outweighs youthful arrogance.

title: Wainamoinen's Wedding-songs
summary: Wainamoinen performs a series of magical wedding songs, using his voice to bless the union of Ilmarinen and the Daughter of Pohjola. His songs provide wisdom to the couple and attempt to instill harmony between the two conflicting nations.
full_text: This legendary tale explores the depths of Finnish folklore. Wainamoinen performs a series of magical wedding songs, using his voice to bless the union of Ilmarinen and the Daughter of Pohjola. His songs provide wisdom to the couple and attempt to instill harmony between the two conflicting nations. The narrative emphasizes the magical nature of the world and the eternal struggle between order and chaos, reflecting the spiritual beliefs of the ancient North.
moral: Ancient wisdom outweighs youthful arrogance.

title: Wainamoinen's Sailing
summary: Wainamoinen organizes a final, daring voyage to retrieve the Sampo from the fortress of Pohjola. He coordinates with other heroes of Kalevala, combining their strengths for one last great effort to secure the source of prosperity.
full_text: This legendary tale explores the depths of Finnish folklore. Wainamoinen organizes a final, daring voyage to retrieve the Sampo from the fortress of Pohjola. He coordinates with other heroes of Kalevala, combining their strengths for one last great effort to secure the source of prosperity. The narrative emphasizes the magical nature of the world and the eternal struggle between order and chaos, reflecting the spiritual beliefs of the ancient North.
moral: Material wealth is fleeting compared to spiritual power.

title: Wainamoinen's Harp-songs
summary: The songs played on the kantele are used to preserve the history of the world and the legendary deeds of the heroes. This process establishes the harp as a central tool for cultural memory and divine communication.
full_text: This legendary tale explores the depths of Finnish folklore. The songs played on the kantele are used to preserve the history of the world and the legendary deeds of the heroes. This process establishes the harp as a central tool for cultural memory and divine communication. The narrative emphasizes the magical nature of the world and the eternal struggle between order and chaos, reflecting the spiritual beliefs of the ancient North.
moral: The balance of nature must be respected.

title: Why the Salt is Far Away
summary: A Hopi legend explaining why the Hopi must travel long distances to obtain salt. Long ago, salt was close to the Hopi villages. But a woman or a group of people violated a taboo involving salt — either by being wasteful, disrespectful, or by using salt improperly during a ceremony. The Salt Woman (or salt spirits) became angry and moved the salt deposits far away, to the Grand Canyon and other distant locations. Ever since, the Hopi have had to undertake long, dangerous journeys to gather salt, a sacred substance used in ceremonies and daily life. The story teaches respect for natural resources and the consequences of taking blessings for granted.
source: Harold Courlander, People of the Short Blue Corn (1993); various Hopi oral traditions

title: White Painted Woman and Child of the Water (Chiricahua Apache)
summary: In Chiricahua and Mescalero Apache tradition, the creation begins not with an emergence from the underworld but with a great flood that destroyed a malevolent world. After the flood, White Painted Woman (Changing Woman) appeared. She conceived Child of the Water (also known as Killer-of-Enemies in Lipan tradition) from the rays of the Sun. Child of the Water became the dominant culture hero who rids the world of monsters. He is sometimes described as the Sun himself in some versions, and his younger brother is the Wise One. White Painted Woman is identified with the Moon and Thunder. The Girls' Puberty Ceremony reenacts White Painted Woman's first menstruation, when she received her power from the Sun. The ceremony lasts four days and includes the Mountain Spirit dance, blessing the young woman with health, fertility, and a long life, just as White Painted Woman was blessed.
source: Morris Edward Opler, Myths and Tales of the Chiricahua Apache Indians (1994); nativeamerican-art.com

title: White Buffalo Calf Woman and the Sacred Pipe
summary: During a time of great famine, two Lakota scouts encountered a mysterious woman in white buckskin. One scout approached her with lustful intent and was reduced to bones. The woman instructed the remaining scout to prepare the tribe. She gave the people the chanupa (sacred pipe) made of red stone carved with a buffalo calf, taught them the Seven Sacred Rites including the Sun Dance and vision quest, and showed them how to pray to Wakan Tanka. Before departing she transformed through four colors—black, red, brown, and white—becoming a white buffalo calf who promised to return when the people needed her most.
source: Wikipedia / Lakota Times / World History Encyclopedia

title: Wihio the Spider Trickster
summary: Wihio is the Cheyenne trickster figure, a spider-like being similar to Iktomi of the Lakota. He is clever but greedy, often getting into trouble because of his selfishness. In one story, Wihio tried to kill some ducks for a feast by tricking them into a dance with their eyes closed. A loon peeked and exposed the trick, and Wihio was shamed. In another tale, Wihio's greed caused him to be trapped inside a hollow log by his own scheme. Wihio stories serve as cautionary tales, teaching Cheyenne children about the consequences of selfish behavior and the importance of community values.

title: Wolf Spirit and the Warrior's Vision
summary: A young Comanche warrior went on a vision quest alone in the mountains, fasting for four days without water. On the fourth night, a Wolf appeared to him in a vision. The Wolf said, 'I am your brother. I will give you strength, courage, and the ability to track your enemies. But you must never kill my kind, for we are connected.' The warrior returned to his people with Wolf as his guardian spirit. He became a great hunter and warrior, always guided by Wolf's wisdom. This story reflects the Comanche belief in animal spirit guides who grant powers to individuals through vision quests.

title: Wenebojo Creates the Earth (After the Great Flood)
summary: In the Algonquian creation story, the trickster-transformer Wenebojo (Nanabozho) caused a great flood through his battle with the underwater serpents who killed his brother. After the waters covered everything, Wenebojo floated on a log with the surviving animals. He asked the loon, then the otter, then the muskrat to dive to the bottom of the flood waters and bring up a pawful of earth. One by one they tried; each failed until the humble muskrat dove so deep he nearly died, but surfaced with a tiny bit of mud in his paw. From this mud, Wenebojo created a new earth, placing it on the back of a great turtle. The turtle grew and grew, becoming Turtle Island, the North American continent. The muskrat's sacrifice is remembered as proof that even the smallest can achieve the greatest.
source: native-languages.org Nanabozho; Johnston, Basil - The Mishomis Book; Leland - Algonquin Legends

title: Wenebojo and the Dancing Geese
summary: Wenebojo, hungry and lazy, saw a flock of geese dancing on the shore. He convinced them to teach him their dance, saying he had seen a more beautiful dance in his dreams. The geese agreed, and Wenebojo joined them, singing and dancing with his eyes closed as he had been instructed. As they danced, Wenebojo began to wring the necks of the geese one by one, still keeping his eyes shut. A young goose opened its eyes and saw what was happening, crying out a warning. The remaining geese flew away. Wenebojo was left with a pile of dead geese, but his treachery was discovered and he faced the consequences. This trickster tale teaches both humor and the lesson that dishonesty will eventually be exposed.

title: Wunzh and the Origin of Indian Corn (Mon-Daw-Min)
summary: A young boy named Wunzh went into the forest to fast and pray for guidance, as was the custom of his people. After many days of fasting, a spirit appeared to him dressed in green and yellow, with feathers on his head. The spirit challenged Wunzh to wrestle him. Though weak from fasting, Wunzh fought with all his strength. The spirit returned every day, and each day Wunzh grew stronger. Finally, the spirit revealed himself as Mon-Daw-Min, the gift of the Creator. He told Wunzh to kill him, bury his body in the ground, and tend the grave. Wunzh obeyed, and from the spirit's body sprouted the first corn plant. The kernels were his teeth, the silk his hair, and the stalk his body. Wunzh brought this sacred knowledge to his people, who have cultivated corn ever since.
source: native-languages.org Chippewa Legends; Schoolcraft, Henry Rowe - Algic Researches

title: Why Leaves Change Color in Autumn
summary: According to Wyandot legend, the changing colors of autumn leaves are the result of a great battle between the summer spirits and the winter spirits. Every autumn, the giant who rules the North Wind comes down from his cold kingdom and fights with the spirit of summer. During the battle, the leaves are bruised and battered, turning yellow, red, and orange from the struggle. The frost giants paint the remaining leaves with ice colors before stripping them from the trees entirely. Another version says that when the Thunders retreat south for the winter, their departing tears fall on the leaves, staining them red and gold. The fallen leaves cover the ground as a blanket to protect Mother Earth during her winter sleep. When spring returns and the Thunders come back north, new green leaves sprout, and the cycle begins again.
source: native-languages.org Wyandot Legends; Barbeau - Huron and Wyandot Mythology

title: Wolf Clan and the Salmon (Tsimshian)
summary: This Tsimshian legend tells of a volcanic eruption caused by disrespect for the salmon. The Wolf Clan, one of the four major Tsimshian clans, had specific protocols for harvesting and honoring the first salmon of the season. One year, a member of the Wolf Clan failed to follow the proper rituals — he did not welcome the salmon chief properly, did not prepare the first salmon with the required ceremony, and showed disrespect to the salmon people. In response, the supernatural forces beneath the mountains grew angry. A great volcanic eruption occurred, devastating the land and reminding all Tsimshian people of the sacred obligation to honor the salmon. The story reinforces the fundamental Northwest Coast principle that humans and salmon are in a reciprocal relationship: humans must treat salmon with respect or face catastrophic consequences. It also explains volcanic activity in the Nass and Skeena River regions through the lens of spiritual cause and effect.

title: Why Mr. Possum Has No Hair on His Tail
summary: A pourquoi tale explaining the possum's hairless tail. Brer Possum once had the most beautiful, bushy tail in the animal kingdom. He was so proud of it that he spent all his time grooming and displaying it, neglecting to gather food for the winter. When winter came, Brer Possum was starving. He went to Brer Rabbit and begged for food. Brer Rabbit, always the trickster, offered him a deal: if Brer Possum would let Brer Rabbit cut off his beautiful tail, Brer Rabbit would give him food for the entire winter. Brer Possum refused at first, but as hunger grew, he reluctantly agreed. Brer Rabbit cut off the bushy tail and gave Brer Possum food. But the tail never grew back, and only a thin, hairless, rat-like tail remained. Brer Possum was so ashamed that he learned to play dead whenever anyone looked at him, so they wouldn't see his shameful tail. To this day, possums have hairless tails and play dead when frightened, eternally reminded of the price of vanity.
source: Uncle Remus: His Songs and His Sayings (Joel Chandler Harris, 1881)

title: Why the Negro Is Black
summary: An African American origin tale explaining skin color, collected by Joel Chandler Harris. According to Uncle Remus, in the beginning, all people were the same color. The Lord was making people and set them out to bake in the sun, like bread. He planned to bake them different shades—some light, some dark, some in between. The first batch was put in the oven, but the Lord became busy with other matters and left them too long. When he took them out, they were burnt quite black. That was the Negro. The second batch, watched more carefully, came out just right—a nice golden brown. That was the Indian. The third batch, watched too nervously, was pulled out too soon and came out pale and undercooked. That was the white man. The story is told with characteristic humor and is typical of African American 'why' tales that use wit to explain natural phenomena, often with a subtle commentary on racial hierarchy. While using self-deprecating humor, the tale subtly subverts racist ideologies by suggesting racial difference is mere accident of timing in the divine kitchen.
source: Uncle Remus: His Songs and His Sayings (Joel Chandler Harris, 1881)

title: Why the Spider Has a Bald Head (Ashanti/Akan)
summary: Anansi attended his mother-in-law's funeral wearing a fine hat made of leopard skin. During the mourning, Anansi became infatuated with the chief's daughter and boasted that he could perform a magical dance. The chief agreed, but warned that if Anansi failed to impress, he would lose his head. Anansi danced wildly, and the hat flew off, revealing his bald head. The chief's daughter laughed at him, and everyone realized his boasting was empty. In shame, Anansi tried to hide but his hat was gone forever. To this day, spiders are bald because of Anansi's vanity and empty boasting. The story teaches the dangers of pride and the importance of humility in Akan culture.
source: Ashanti oral tradition, R.S. Rattray collection

title: Why Men Commit Evil at Night (Ashanti/Akan)
summary: Nyame the Sky God had three sons: Esum (Darkness), Osrane (Moon), and Owia (Sun). He wanted to make Owia a chief and proposed that the son who could guess the name of a secret yam would inherit his royal stool. Anansi discovered the name 'Kintinkyi' and revealed it to Owia, who won the contest. Nyame punished Esum by decreeing that evil deeds would happen in his time (night). He told Osrane that only children would play during his time (moonlight). And he blessed Owia (daytime) as the time for settling disputes and doing good work. Nyame gave Owia the rainbow as a promise of protection from his brothers, and he made Anansi his messenger for knowing his thoughts. This story explains the division of day and night and why light and dark have different moral qualities in Akan cosmology.
source: Ashanti oral tradition, R.S. Rattray collection

title: Were and the Creation of the World
summary: Were, the supreme god of the Luo, created the world from the primordial waters. First, Were created the sky and the earth, then the sun, moon, and stars to give light. He fashioned the first man and woman from clay and breathed life into their nostrils. Were gave them cattle, goats, and seeds to plant, teaching them how to live. But the first people disobeyed Were's command not to eat from a certain tree, and death entered the world. Were withdrew from direct contact with humanity but left the ancestral spirits (juogi) as intermediaries. The juogi watch over their descendants, guiding them and protecting them from harm. To this day, the Luo honor Were through prayers and offerings made at dawn and dusk, when the boundary between the physical and spiritual worlds grows thin.
moral: Disobedience has consequences, but the divine remains ever-present through ancestral guidance.

title: Walumbe: The Origin of Death
summary: Walumbe, whose name means 'death' in Luganda, was the brother of Nambi who followed her and Kintu to earth when they forgot the millet. Ggulu had warned them not to return, but Walumbe insisted on accompanying them on their way back. Once on earth, Walumbe demanded one of Kintu and Nambi's children as his servant. Kintu refused, and Walumbe began killing the children one by one. Kintu tried to hide his children in a deep trench, but Walumbe's power was inescapable. Kintu appealed to Ggulu, who sent his other sons to capture Walumbe. They caught him and were dragging him back to the sky, but Walumbe tricked them by crying out and startling them into letting go. He escaped into a deep hole in the earth from which he emerged periodically to claim his due. And so death became permanent in the world. Yet Ggulu decreed that Walumbe could only take one person at a time, and that new life would always replace the old, ensuring the continuation of humanity.
source: Ganda oral tradition, Buganda kingdom folklore
moral: Death is an inescapable part of life, but life itself continues endlessly.

title: Wandjina the Cloud Spirits
summary: The Wandjina (also Wanjina, Wondjina) are powerful cloud and rain spirits in the beliefs of the Wanjina Wunggurr cultural bloc of northwestern Australia, including the Worrorra, Wunambal, and Ngarinyin peoples of the Kimberley region. According to Dreamtime legends, the Wandjina created the landscape and its inhabitants and continue to influence both. When the spirit beings found the place where they would die, they painted their images on cave walls and entered a nearby waterhole. The paintings, which date back about 4,000 years, show figures with large upper bodies, eyes and noses but typically no mouths (because they are so powerful they do not need speech, and if they had mouths rain would never cease). The Wandjina can punish lawbreakers with floods, lightning, and cyclones. Aboriginal people repaint these images annually to regenerate their life-force and ensure the coming of monsoon rains.
